name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱلشَّمْسِ وَضُحَىٰهَا (١)
```

```english
By the sun and her forenoon splendour, (1)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْقَمَرِ إِذَا تَلَىٰهَا (٢)
```

```english
by the moon when he follows her, (2)
```

```arabic
وَٱلنَّهَارِ إِذَا جَلَّىٰهَا (٣)
```

```english
by the day when it reveals her, (3)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَغْشَىٰهَا (٤)
```

```english
by the night when it covers her, (4)
```

```arabic
وَٱلسَّمَآءِ وَمَا بَنَىٰهَا (٥)
```

```english
by the sky and Him who built it, (5)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا طَحَىٰهَا (٦)
```

```english
by the earth and Him who spread it, (6)
```

```arabic
وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّىٰهَا (٧)
```

```english
by the soul and Him who fashioned it, (7)
```

```arabic
فَأَلْهَمَهَا فُجُورَهَا وَتَقْوَىٰهَا (٨)
```

```english
and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices: (8)
```

```arabic
قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَن زَكَّىٰهَا (٩)
```

```english
one who purifies it is felicitous, (9)
```

```arabic
وَقَدْ خَابَ مَن دَسَّىٰهَا (١٠)
```

```english
and one who betrays it fails. (10)
```

```arabic
كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِطَغْوَىٰهَآ (١١)
```

```english
The [people of] Thamud denied [Allah’s signs] out of their rebellion, (11)
```

```arabic
إِذِ ٱنۢبَعَثَ أَشْقَىٰهَا (١٢)
```

```english
when the most wretched of them rose up. (12)
```

```arabic
فَقَالَ لَهُمْ رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ نَاقَةَ ٱللَّهِ وَسُقْيَـٰهَا (١٣)
```

```english
The apostle of Allah had told them, ‘This is Allah’s she-camel, let her drink!’ (13)
```

```arabic
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَعَقَرُوهَا فَدَمْدَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُم بِذَنۢبِهِمْ فَسَوَّىٰهَا (١٤)
```

```english
But they impugned him and hamstrung her. So their Lord took them unawares by night because of their sin, and levelled it. (14)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَخَافُ عُقْبَـٰهَا (١٥)
```

```english
And He does not fear its outcome. (15)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah mentions that the success of man—who recognizes godwariness (التقوى) and wickedness (الفجور) through divine definition and inner inspiration (إلهام باطني)—lies in purifying his soul and developing it through a righteous growth (إنماء صالح) by adorning it with godwariness and cleansing it from wickedness. Conversely, failure and deprivation from felicity await the one who stunts it (يدسيها). To illustrate this, the Surah cites the example of what befell the people of Thamud—the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال)—when they denied their messenger, Salih, and hamstrung the she-camel. In this, there is an allusion to the people of Makkah. The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by its context (سياق).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالشَّمْسِ وَضُحاها** **By the sun and her forenoon brightness** (91:1). In *al-Mufradat*, it is stated: "The *duha* (الضحى) is the spreading of the sun and the extension of the day; the time was named after it." End quote. The pronoun refers to the sun. The verse contains an oath by the sun and the spreading of its light upon the earth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْقَمَرِ إِذا تَلاها** **by the moon when he follows her** (91:2). This is coordinated with "the sun," and the pronoun refers to it. It is an oath by the moon in the state of following the sun. What is meant by its following the sun is either its acquisition of light from it—in which case the state is a permanent one—or its rising after the sun's setting, in which case the oath refers to its state from being a crescent until it becomes a full moon.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالنَّهارِ إِذا جَلَّاها** **by the day when he brightens her** (91:3). *Tajliyah* (التجلية) means manifestation (الإظهار) and bringing to light (الإبراز). The feminine pronoun refers to the earth. The meaning is: "I swear by the day when it manifests the earth to the eyes."

It has been said that the pronoun of the subject in **جَلَّاها** refers to the day, while the object pronoun refers to the sun. The meaning would then be an oath by the state of the day manifesting the sun, for the sun becomes clear and appears when the day spreads. However, this is problematic as it does not align with what preceded it; for it is the sun that manifests the day, not the reverse.

It has also been said that the feminine pronoun refers to the world (الدنيا), or to the darkness (الظلمة). Others say the subject pronoun refers to Allah, the Exalted, and the object pronoun to the sun, meaning: "I swear by the day when Allah manifests the sun." These are far-fetched views.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشاها** **by the night when he enshrouds her** (91:4). That is, it covers the earth. Thus, the pronoun refers to the earth, as in **جَلَّاها**.

It has been said it refers to the sun, but this is far-fetched, for the night does not cover the sun; rather, it covers the earth and what is upon it.

The use of the present tense (المضارع) for the night's enshrouding of the earth, in contrast to the past tense used for the day's brightening of it—where it says: **وَالنَّهارِ إِذا جَلَّاها وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشاها**—is to indicate the present state (الحال). This may be an allusion to the enshrouding of wickedness (الفجور) over the earth in the present time, which was the early period of the Islamic call (الدعوة الإسلامية), since it was previously mentioned that there is a type of connection and correlation between these oaths and the things sworn by. This is in addition to maintaining the rhythmic endings (الفواصل).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالسَّماءِ وَما بَناها وَالْأَرْضِ وَما طَحاها** **by the sky and Him who built it, by the earth and Him who spread it** (91:5-6). The *tahw* (طحو) of the earth and its *dahw* (دحو) mean its spreading out. The word **ما** (what/who) in **وَما بَناها** and **ما طَحاها** is a relative pronoun (موصولة). The One who built it and spread it is Allah, the Exalted. The use of **ما** (what) instead of **من** (who) is to prefer ambiguity (الإبهام) which serves to emphasize greatness (التفخيم) and evoke wonder (التعجيب). Thus, the meaning is: "I swear by the heaven and the Mighty, Wondrous Being who built it; and I swear by the earth and the Mighty, Wondrous Being who spread it."

It has been said that **ما** is an infinitive particle (مصدرية), meaning: "I swear by the heaven and its building, and the earth and its spreading." However, the context—which includes His saying: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها** etc.—does not support this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها** **by the soul and Him who fashioned it** (91:7). That is: "I swear by a soul and the Being possessed of power, knowledge, and wisdom who fashioned it, arranged its creation, organized its limbs, and balanced its faculties."

Regarding the indefiniteness (التنكير) of **نَفْسٍ** (a soul), it has been said it is for generalization, and others say it is for magnification (التفخيم). It is not unlikely that the indefiniteness is to indicate that it possesses a unique description and a significant matter.

What is meant by "the soul" is the human soul (النفس الإنسانية) in an absolute sense. It has been said it refers to the soul of Adam (peace be upon him), but the context does not suit this, especially His saying: **قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها**, unless it is used by way of synecdoche (الاستخدام), though there is no reason for such specification.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها** **and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness** (91:8). Wickedness (الفجور) — *the act of tearing the veil of religiosity* — as mentioned by al-Raghib, implies that the divine prohibition against an act or omission is a drawn veil and a barrier between man and that act; committing the prohibited is a tearing of that veil and a breach of that barrier.

Godwariness (التقوى) — *the act of placing the soul in protection against what is feared* — as mentioned by al-Raghib, refers here, by the context of its juxtaposition with wickedness, to the avoidance of wickedness and guarding against what is contrary to righteousness. It has been interpreted in narrations as "piety (الورع) regarding the prohibitions of Allah."

Inspiration (الإلهام) — *the casting of a matter into the heart* — is Allah's bestowing upon the soul practical forms (الصور العملية), whether in the form of conceptualization (تصور) or assent (تصديق).

The linking of inspiration to the titles of the soul's "wickedness" and "godwariness" indicates that what is meant is Allah's making known to man the quality of his action—whether it be godwariness or wickedness—beyond making known the essence of the act itself in its primary, neutral title. For example, "consuming wealth" is common to both consuming the orphan's wealth, which is wickedness (فجور), and consuming one's own wealth, which is godwariness (تقوى). Similarly, "physical intimacy" is common to both adultery, which is wickedness, and marriage, which is godwariness. In short, the meaning is that Allah has made man recognize whether the act he performs is wickedness or godwariness, and has distinguished for him what is godwariness from what is wickedness.

The branching of inspiration from "fashioning" (التسوية) in His saying: **وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها** etc., is to indicate that the inspiration of wickedness and godwariness—which is the practical intellect (العقل العملي)—is part of the completion of the soul's fashioning. Thus, it is among the attributes of its creation (الخلقة), as He, the Exalted, said: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** **So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature [and religion] of Allah according to which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion** (30:30).

The attribution of wickedness and godwariness to the pronoun of the soul is to indicate that what is meant by the inspired wickedness and godwariness are those specific to this mentioned soul, which is the human soul and the souls of the Jinn, as appears from the Mighty Book regarding their being charged with faith and righteous deeds.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها** **one who purifies it is certainly felicitous, and one who betrays it has certainly failed** (91:9-10). Success (الفلاح) is the attainment of the desired and the reaching of one's goal. Failure (الخيبة) is its opposite. *Zakat* (الزكاة) is the righteous and blessed growth of a plant, and *tazkiyah* (التزكية) is to cause it to grow in such a manner. *Tadasi* (التدسي)—which comes from *al-dass* (الدس) by changing one of the two *sins* into a *ya*—is to insert something into another with a degree of concealment. By the context of its juxtaposition with *tazkiyah*, it means causing growth in a manner contrary to what its nature (طبع) requires and what its soul was fashioned upon.

The verse, namely His saying: **قَدْ أَفْلَحَ** etc., is the response to the oath (جواب القسم), and His saying: **وَقَدْ خابَ** etc., is coordinated with it.

The use of "purification" (التزكية) and "stunting/concealment" (التدسي) to describe the soul's rectification or corruption is based on what is indicated by His saying: **فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها**, namely that part of the perfection of the human soul is that it is inspired and capable of distinguishing—by its primordial nature (الفطرة)—wickedness from godwariness. That is, the religion (الدين)—which is submission (الإسلام) to Allah in what He wills—is innate (فطري) to the soul. Thus, adorning the soul with godwariness is a purification (تزكية) and a righteous growth, providing it with that which sustains its survival. He, the Exalted, said: **وَتَزَوَّدُوا فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ الزَّادِ التَّقْوى وَاتَّقُونِ يا أُولِي الْأَلْبابِ** **And take provision, for indeed the best provision is godwariness. So be wary of Me, O you who possess intellects** (2:197). The matter of wickedness is the opposite of godwariness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ بِطَغْواها** **The [people of] Thamud denied [their prophet] because of their rebellion** (91:11). *Al-taghwa* (الطغوى) is a verbal noun like *al-tughyan* (rebellion/transgression), and the particle *ba* denotes causality (السببية).

This verse and those following it until the end of the Surah serve as an illustration and confirmation of what preceded in His saying: **قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها** etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذِ انْبَعَثَ أَشْقاها** **when the most wretched of them rose up** (91:12). This is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) for His saying **كَذَّبَتْ** or for **بِطَغْواها**. What is meant by the "most wretched of Thamud" is the one who hamstrung the she-camel; his name, according to narrations, was Qudar ibn Salif. His rising up was by the instigation of the people, as indicated by the following verses which contain plural pronouns.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَقالَ لَهُمْ رَسُولُ اللهِ ناقَةَ اللهِ وَسُقْياها** **and the apostle of Allah said to them, ‘[Let alone] the she-camel of Allah and her drink’** (91:13). What is meant by "the apostle of Allah" is Salih (peace be upon him), the prophet of Thamud. His saying **ناقَةَ اللهِ** is in the accusative case for warning (التحذير), and **وَسُقْياها** is coordinated with it.

The meaning is: Salih said to them, carrying a message from Allah: "Beware of the she-camel of Allah and her drink; do not interfere with her by killing her or preventing her from her turn to drink water." Allah has detailed this story in Surah Hud and elsewhere.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَعَقَرُوها فَدَمْدَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ بِذَنْبِهِمْ فَسَوَّاها** **But they denied him and hamstrung her, so their Lord destroyed them for their sin and leveled it [with the ground]** (91:14). *Al-aqr* (العقر) is to strike the root of a thing, and it is used for slaughtering a camel or killing. *Al-damdamah* (الدمدمة) upon something is to enclose it completely; it is said: "the grave *damdama* over him," meaning it enclosed him. What is meant is their being encompassed by a punishment that cuts off their progeny and erases their traces because of their sin.

Regarding His saying: **فَسَوَّاها**, the apparent meaning is that the pronoun refers to Thamud, considering them as a tribe; i.e., He leveled them with the earth. Or it refers to the leveling of the earth, meaning its flattening and the removal of its elevations and depressions.

It has also been said that the pronoun refers to the *damdamah* (destruction) understood from His saying **فَدَمْدَمَ**, meaning He made the destruction equal among them, such that neither the strong nor the weak, nor the old nor the young, escaped.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا يَخافُ عُقْباها** **and He does not fear its outcome** (91:15). The pronoun refers to the destruction (الدمدمة) or the leveling (التسوية). The *waw* is for commencement (الاستئناف) or state (الحال).

The meaning is: Their Lord does not fear the consequence of the destruction upon them and their leveling, as kings and the powerful fear the consequences and repercussions of punishing their enemies. This is because the outcomes of all matters are what He wills and occur according to His permission. Thus, the verse is close in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: **لا يُسْئَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْئَلُونَ** **He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned** (21:23).

It has been said that the pronoun in **لا يَخافُ** refers to the "most wretched one," meaning the one who hamstrung the she-camel did not fear the outcome of what he did to her.

It has also been said that the pronoun in **لا يَخافُ** refers to Salih, and the pronoun in **عُقْباها** refers to the destruction, meaning Salih did not fear the outcome of the destruction upon them due to his certainty of salvation. The weakness of these two views is obvious.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها**, he said: "He created it and fashioned it."

In *al-Majma'*, it is narrated that Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim reported from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها**, that they said: "He made clear to it what to perform and what to leave." And regarding His saying, the Exalted: **قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها**, they said: "He has succeeded who obeyed," and **وَقَدْ خابَ مَنْ دَسَّاها**, they said: "He has failed who disobeyed."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, Muslim, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Imran ibn Husayn that a man said: "O Messenger of Allah, do you see what people do today and toil in—is it something that has been decreed for them and passed upon them in a destiny (قدر) that preceded? Or is it in what they will face from their Prophet and for which the proof has been established against them?" He said: "Rather, it is something decreed for them."

He asked: "Then why do they act?" He replied: "Whomever Allah created for one of the two stations, He prepares him for its work—and the confirmation of that is in the Book of Allah: **وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها**."

**I say:** His saying: "Or is it in what they will face..."—it appears that the *hamzah* is for interrogation and the *waw* for coordination. The meaning is: "And is there in their obedience to their Prophet a decree from Allah and a destiny that has preceded?" His saying: "Then why do they act?" means: "What then is the meaning of their action and the attribution of the act to them?"

The saying of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Whomever Allah..." means that the necessity of the act's issuance—whether good or evil—from them in view of the prior decree and destiny (القضاء والقدر) does not negate the possibility of its issuance in view of the human being and his choice (اختياره). This has been clarified in previous discussions in this book repeatedly.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Daylami narrated from Juwaybir from al-Dahhak from Ibn 'Abbas: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: **قَدْ أَفْلَحَ مَنْ زَكَّاها**—the verse—'Successful is the soul that Allah has purified, and failed is the soul that Allah has deprived of all good.'"

**I say:** The attribution of purification (التزكية) and deprivation (التخييب) to Him, the Exalted, in one respect does not negate their attribution to the human being through obedience and disobedience.

Indeed, only that misguidance (الإضلال) which is by way of requital (المجازاة) is attributed to Allah, the Glorified, as He said: **وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** **and He misleads none by it except the transgressors** (2:26).

In *al-Majma'*, a narration is authenticated through a chain from 'Uthman ibn Suhayb from his father, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to 'Ali ibn Abi Talib: 'Who is the most wretched of the ancients?' He said: 'The one who hamstrung the she-camel.' He said: 'You have spoken the truth. Then who is the most wretched of the later ones?' He said: 'I said: I do not know, O Messenger of Allah.' He said: 'The one who strikes you upon this'—and he pointed to his crown (يافوخة)." **I say:** This meaning is also narrated therein from 'Ammar ibn Yasir.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*: al-Tha'labi and al-Wahidi narrated with their chains from 'Ammar, from 'Uthman ibn Suhayb, and from al-Dahhak. Ibn Mardawayh narrated with his chain from Jabir ibn Samurah, from 'Ammar, and from Ibn 'Adi or from al-Dahhak. Al-Khatib narrated in *al-Tarikh* from Jabir ibn Samurah. Al-Tabari and al-Mawsili narrated, and Ahmad narrated from al-Dahhak from 'Ammar that he said: "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'O 'Ali, the most wretched of the ancients was the one who hamstrung the she-camel, and the most wretched of the later ones is your killer.' In one narration: 'The one who dyes this from this.'"
